heroOverview: Kwashiorkor is a severe form of acute protein malnutrition characterised by pitting oedema — swelling caused by fluid leaking into tissues — that paradoxically masks an underlying state of extreme nutrient depletion. It differs from marasmus, in which wasting is obvious, because the oedema can make the body appear deceptively normal in weight while visceral protein stores and immune function are catastrophically depleted. Kwashiorkor accounts for a significant proportion of malnutrition-related mortality in sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and conflict-affected regions, with WHO estimating that severe acute malnutrition affects roughly 17 million children under 5 globally at any given time. Case fatality rates in hospital series range from 5 to 30%, dropping substantially when a standardised management protocol including F-75 and F-100 therapeutic feeds and treatment of infections is followed.

keyStats.prevalence: Severe acute malnutrition, of which kwashiorkor is a major subtype, affects an estimated 17 million children under 5 globally at any one time (WHO, 2024)
keyStats.demographics: Predominantly affects children aged 1–5 in low-income, food-insecure settings; also occurs in adults with chronic illness, alcoholism, or severe dietary restriction
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in children 12–36 months, typically at or after weaning from breast milk
keyStats.globalCases: Global mortality from severe acute malnutrition contributes to approximately 2.3 million child deaths annually, with kwashiorkor a major contributor

definition: Kwashiorkor (ICD-10: E40) is a syndrome of severe protein-energy malnutrition defined clinically by the presence of bilateral pitting oedema in a child or adult who is also malnourished, regardless of weight-for-height. The name derives from a Ghanaian Ga language word meaning 'the disease the child gets when another baby is born' — referencing the traditional practice of weaning an older child onto a starchy, protein-poor diet when the next infant displaces them from the breast. The pathophysiology involves multiple converging mechanisms: severely inadequate dietary protein drives down serum albumin and other visceral proteins (reducing plasma oncotic pressure and causing oedema), while antioxidant deficiencies including selenium, zinc, and vitamins A and E impair the capacity to neutralise reactive oxygen species generated by infection or contaminated complementary foods. The gut mucosa becomes atrophied and permeable, promoting bacterial translocation and further immune dysfunction. Metabolic adaptation, hormonal disruption (particularly of insulin and insulin-like growth factor 1), and micronutrient depletion combine to impair every organ system. The liver accumulates fat (fatty liver of kwashiorkor), the skin and hair develop pathognomonic changes, and the intestine is unable to absorb the nutrients that are provided. WHO and UNICEF protocols classify kwashiorkor under severe acute malnutrition and mandate inpatient treatment in the initial stabilisation phase.

typesClassification.description: Severe protein malnutrition with bilateral pitting oedema as the defining feature; weight-for-height may appear normal or near-normal because oedema masks underlying wasting.
The combined form, ICD-10 E42, where oedema typical of kwashiorkor coexists with severe visible wasting from marasmus; carries the highest case fatality rate among acute malnutrition subtypes.

Occurs in adults with prolonged protein-deficient diets, alcoholism, extreme restrictive eating, inflammatory bowel disease, or conditions that impair protein absorption; clinical features are similar but oedema may be attributed initially to other causes.

primarySymptoms: Bilateral pitting oedema, the hallmark sign, beginning in the feet and ankles and ascending to the legs, hands, face, and trunk as severity increases; it reflects reduced plasma oncotic pressure from hypoalbuminaemia.
Moon face (facial oedema) giving a rounded appearance, often misread by caregivers as a sign of good health and causing delayed presentation.
Skin changes — hypopigmentation (pale patches), darkening, peeling, and desquamation in a 'flaky paint' or 'crazy-paving' pattern affecting the legs, buttocks, and perineum.

Hair changes — loss of normal pigmentation producing reddish or yellowish discolouration, easy plucking without pain (the 'flag sign' when alternating bands of normal and pale hair mark episodes of poor nutrition), and sparse, thin, or straight hair in previously curly-haired children.
Hepatomegaly from fatty infiltration of the liver (hepatic steatosis), detected as a palpable, non-tender liver edge below the right costal margin.
Severe anorexia and food refusal, profoundly complicating treatment as the child resists the very feeds needed for recovery.
Irritability, misery, and marked psychological withdrawal — the clinical state often called 'miserable' kwashiorkor — contrasting with the hyperactive irritability of other febrile states.
Muscle wasting that is masked by oedema but evident on examination of the arms, buttocks, and calves once oedema begins to resolve.
Impaired immune function leading to recurrent or concurrent infections — pneumonia, measles, gastroenteritis, tuberculosis, and sepsis are common precipitants and complications.
Oedematous changes in the gastrointestinal tract causing malabsorption, diarrhoea, and abdominal distension from intestinal wall oedema, bacterial overgrowth, and mucosal atrophy.
earlyWarningSigns: A child who stops gaining weight or begins to lose weight during or after weaning from breast milk.
Mild ankle swelling that persists over several days in a child from a food-insecure setting.
Gradual loss of the normal shine and curl of hair with early colour changes to reddish-brown.
Increased fussiness, reduced play activity, and progressive disinterest in food over days to weeks.
emergencySigns: Severe oedema extending to the face, arms, and genitals with shock signs (cold extremities, altered consciousness, weak rapid pulse) indicating circulatory failure.
Hypoglycaemia (blood glucose below 3 mmol/L), which is common in severe malnutrition and can cause seizures and death if not treated immediately with oral or intravenous glucose.
Hypothermia (core temperature below 35°C), a sign of critically impaired thermoregulation requiring immediate warming alongside nutritional treatment.
Rapid breathing or grunting suggesting pneumonia or heart failure in the context of aggressive fluid resuscitation.
Worsening consciousness or unresponsiveness requiring emergency assessment and treatment for sepsis, hypoglycaemia, and electrolyte imbalance.

causes.cause: Severely inadequate dietary protein with relative carbohydrate preservation
causes.description: The classical nutritional cause is a diet providing energy mainly from starchy staples (cassava, yam, maize porridge, plantain) with minimal protein from legumes, meat, dairy, or fish; the protein deficit is disproportionate to energy deficit, distinguishing kwashiorkor's oedematous presentation from the dry wasting of marasmus caused by global caloric insufficiency.

causes.cause: Abrupt weaning onto protein-poor complementary foods
causes.description: When breastfeeding ends — voluntarily or because of a new pregnancy — the older child is frequently introduced to energy-dense but protein-poor weaning foods; the transition from breast milk's balanced protein to a starch-dominant diet is the classic precipitant in the 12–36-month age group.

Each episode of gastroenteritis, respiratory infection, measles, or malaria substantially increases protein catabolism and micronutrient losses while reducing appetite and food intake; in a child already near the margin of nutritional adequacy, a single infection can push them into frank kwashiorkor within days.

Evidence suggests that deficiencies of selenium, zinc, vitamin A, and vitamin E impair the ability to neutralise reactive oxygen species; additionally, aflatoxin contamination of improperly stored maize and groundnuts may contribute by damaging hepatic antioxidant defences, helping explain why kwashiorkor clusters in certain communities despite similar protein intake.

causes.cause: Chronic illness impeding protein absorption or increasing requirements
causes.description: In adults, inflammatory bowel disease (particularly Crohn's disease), short bowel syndrome, chronic alcoholism, and advanced malignancy cause protein-losing enteropathy or chronic hypercatabolism that can produce kwashiorkor-like protein deficiency states even in higher-income settings.

Structural determinants — drought, conflict, poverty, displacement, and disrupted food systems — determine population-level kwashiorkor risk; communities depending on monoculture staple crops with little dietary diversity are most vulnerable, particularly during agricultural or political shocks that further reduce food access.

riskFactors.factor: Age 6–36 months during or after weaning
riskFactors.description: This developmental window coincides with the shift from high-quality breast milk protein to complementary foods; rapid growth and immune immaturity at this age make protein demands high while dietary variety is often poor.

Families without reliable access to diverse, protein-containing foods — particularly animal-source foods and legumes — are unable to provide adequate protein intake even when carbohydrate-energy needs are met.

riskFactors.factor: Frequent infections, particularly diarrhoea and respiratory illness
riskFactors.description: Recurrent infections greatly increase protein catabolism and nutrient losses; vaccination, safe water, handwashing, and sanitation are preventive measures that reduce infection burden and thereby protect nutritional status.

riskFactors.factor: Dietary monotony and reliance on starchy staples
riskFactors.description: Communities dependent on cassava, plantain, or maize as the sole complementary food provide energy but negligible protein; dietary diversification programmes reduce kwashiorkor risk.

Measles is a major precipitant of severe acute malnutrition; the infection causes rapid protein catabolism, mucosal damage, immune suppression, and appetite loss that can precipitate kwashiorkor in a nutritionally vulnerable child.

riskFactors.factor: Low maternal education and early weaning practices
riskFactors.description: Shorter duration of exclusive breastfeeding and early introduction of non-nutritious liquids reduces protein intake before adequate complementary feeding is established; maternal nutrition education significantly reduces incidence.
Deficiencies of zinc, selenium, vitamin A, and vitamin C impair mucosal integrity, immune defence, and antioxidant protection; these micronutrient gaps are routinely addressed in treatment protocols.

affectedDemographics: Children aged 1–5 years in sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and the Caribbean
Children in conflict zones, refugee settings, and drought-affected communities
Children living in extreme poverty with limited dietary diversity
Adults with severe alcoholism, chronic intestinal malabsorption, or advanced cancer in any country
People in resource-limited settings who have received prolonged low-protein hospital feeding
Elderly individuals in care settings with severe appetite loss and protein-deficient diets
diagnosisOverview: The clinical diagnosis of kwashiorkor is made at the bedside by the presence of bilateral pitting oedema extending at least to both ankles in a child or adult with a history consistent with protein-energy malnutrition — no laboratory test is required to begin treatment. WHO diagnostic criteria for severe acute malnutrition include the presence of oedema together with weight-for-height or weight-for-length below -3 Z-scores, or a mid-upper-arm circumference (MUAC) below 115 mm in children aged 6–59 months. MUAC measurement with a coloured tape is the principal community-level screening tool because it is fast, requires no weighing scale, and has been validated against outcomes. Laboratory investigations are used to identify complications rather than to confirm the nutritional diagnosis: blood glucose for hypoglycaemia, a malaria rapid test in endemic areas, a haemoglobin or full blood count for anaemia, serum electrolytes (particularly potassium, sodium, and phosphate) to guide refeeding, and stool examination for intestinal parasites. Serum albumin is low in kwashiorkor but is not a sensitive diagnostic test because it falls late and is influenced by inflammation; it may be useful for monitoring recovery in hospital settings. Chest X-ray is performed if pneumonia is suspected. HIV testing is conducted routinely in endemic settings because HIV significantly worsens the prognosis of severe malnutrition.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The primary community-level screening test for severe acute malnutrition; a MUAC below 115 mm in children 6–59 months indicates severe acute malnutrition requiring immediate referral.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A flexible tape is placed around the upper arm midway between the shoulder and elbow; the reading takes less than one minute and requires no laboratory equipment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The clinical hallmark of kwashiorkor; firm thumb pressure to both lower legs for 3 seconds leaves a pit in oedematous tissue, distinguishing kwashiorkor from marasmus.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A bedside clinical examination performed by a health worker; graded from + (both feet) to +++ (generalised oedema including face and arms).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects hypoglycaemia — common and life-threatening in severe malnutrition — enabling immediate treatment with glucose before any further assessment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A finger-prick blood drop tested on a glucometer within seconds; performed immediately on arrival at the health facility in every child with severe malnutrition.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Guides intravenous fluid therapy and identifies the dangerous electrolyte shifts that cause refeeding syndrome, cardiac arrhythmia, and sudden death during nutritional rehabilitation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw; results take 1–4 hours. Management is guided by results, particularly before aggressive feeding is started.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies malaria as a concurrent or precipitating cause in endemic areas; untreated malaria substantially worsens protein catabolism and mortality.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A finger-prick blood test returning a result within 15–20 minutes; routinely performed in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects anaemia, which is near-universal in kwashiorkor from combined iron, folate, and vitamin A deficiency, and identifies signs of infection or haematological compromise.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw with results in 30–60 minutes in most facility laboratories.

diagnosticTests.purpose: HIV co-infection dramatically worsens prognosis and requires antiretroviral treatment alongside nutritional rehabilitation; WHO recommends routine HIV testing in all severely malnourished children in endemic settings.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A simple rapid immunoassay from a finger-prick or venous blood sample with results in 20–30 minutes.

treatmentOverview: WHO and UNICEF have developed a standardised 10-step inpatient protocol for severe acute malnutrition that consistently reduces case fatality rates from over 20% to below 5% when followed correctly. The protocol has two phases. Phase 1 (stabilisation, typically days 1–7) treats life-threatening complications first: hypoglycaemia is corrected immediately with 50 mL of 10% glucose; hypothermia is prevented with skin-to-skin warming; infections are treated with empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics (typically amoxicillin; gentamicin for severely ill children); dehydration is corrected with WHO ReSoMal (reduced osmolarity oral rehydration solution for malnutrition) at low volumes — intravenous fluids are avoided because the heart cannot handle volume loads in the context of protein depletion; and micronutrients are provided daily. Feeding during stabilisation uses F-75, a low-protein, low-sodium, low-lactose therapeutic formula providing 75 kcal/100 mL, given in small frequent amounts to avoid overloading a compromised gut and heart. F-75 is deliberately not designed to restore weight but to stabilise physiology and allow oedema to begin resolving. Phase 2 (rehabilitation) begins when oedema diminishes, appetite returns, and complications resolve — typically by day 7–10. F-100 (100 kcal/100 mL, higher protein) or ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF, a peanut-based paste) is introduced progressively under catch-up feeding principles targeting 150–220 kcal/kg/day. Recovery in the community phase uses RUTF supplied through outpatient therapeutic programmes once the child is clinically stable. All children receive a standardised micronutrient package: folic acid on day 1, then a combined supplement providing vitamin A, zinc, iron (from day 15), copper, and a B-complex. Stimulation and emotional support are provided throughout to address the psychological withdrawal that accompanies severe malnutrition. The total inpatient course lasts 2–6 weeks, followed by community outpatient follow-up.

medicalTreatments.name: F-75 therapeutic formula (stabilisation phase)
medicalTreatments.description: A WHO-formulated low-energy (75 kcal/100 mL), low-protein, low-sodium, lactose-reduced formula for initial inpatient feeding; given in 8–12 small feeds per day to avoid overloading the compromised cardiovascular system and gut.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mortality from over 20% to under 5% when used as part of the 10-step protocol; oedema typically begins resolving within 4–7 days.

medicalTreatments.name: Ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF) — Plumpy'Nut or equivalent
medicalTreatments.description: A peanut-based, energy-dense, micronutrient-fortified paste providing approximately 500 kcal per sachet; used during rehabilitation and outpatient phases because it is shelf-stable without refrigeration, requires no mixing, and can be self-administered.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves nutritional recovery (weight-for-height above -2 Z-score) in approximately 75–85% of severe acute malnutrition cases managed in community outpatient programmes.

medicalTreatments.name: Broad-spectrum antibiotics (empiric)
medicalTreatments.description: Every severely malnourished child with any signs of infection receives empiric antibiotics on admission; WHO recommends oral amoxicillin for uncomplicated cases and IV gentamicin plus ampicillin for shocked or severely ill children, because clinical signs of infection are often absent even with bacteraemia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Antibiotic treatment reduces mortality in severe acute malnutrition by approximately 30–40% compared with no antibiotic; routine use regardless of visible infection signs is supported by multiple trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Micronutrient supplementation package
medicalTreatments.description: A standardised course including folic acid on day 1, then daily zinc, copper, combined vitamin and mineral supplement; iron is deferred until after day 14 to avoid providing iron during active infection, when it promotes bacterial growth and oxidative stress.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: The full WHO micronutrient protocol is integral to achieving recovery and correcting the deficiencies that maintain oxidative stress and impaired immune function; zinc supplementation alone reduces diarrhoea duration by approximately 25%.

medicalTreatments.name: ReSoMal (reduced-osmolarity ORS for malnutrition)
medicalTreatments.description: A modified oral rehydration solution with lower sodium content than standard ORS, used in small, slow volumes for dehydration in severe malnutrition to avoid precipitating fluid overload, heart failure, and dangerous hyponatraemia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Safely corrects dehydration in malnourished children at a rate that avoids overload; standard ORS is not used because it provides too much sodium to the compromised kidneys and heart of kwashiorkor patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Community-based therapeutic care and outpatient RUTF programmes
alternativeTreatments.description: The majority of children with severe acute malnutrition who have appetite and no severe complications can be safely treated at home with weekly RUTF supplies and community health worker follow-up; this approach has dramatically expanded treatment coverage in resource-limited settings.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary diversification and social protection
alternativeTreatments.description: Community-level prevention through complementary feeding promotion, kitchen gardens, small animal husbandry, and social safety net programmes reduces kwashiorkor incidence at population scale, though these are prevention strategies rather than treatments for established disease.

whySeeSpecialist: Any child with bilateral pitting oedema and signs of severe malnutrition requires immediate referral to an inpatient facility with a WHO-trained team. A paediatrician or paediatric nutritionist experienced in severe acute malnutrition management is essential to navigate the dangerous initial stabilisation phase, where overcautious and overcorrection of fluids and electrolytes are equally deadly. In high-income settings, a clinical dietitian and gastroenterologist involvement may be needed for adult kwashiorkor from chronic intestinal disease.
doctorSelectionGuide: In low-resource settings, seek a WHO-certified Therapeutic Feeding Centre with trained nursing staff, reliable F-75/F-100 and RUTF supplies, and 24-hour blood glucose monitoring capability. In higher-income countries, a paediatric hospital with subspecialty nutrition support services and a multidisciplinary team — paediatric dietitian, gastroenterologist, and social worker — provides the best outcomes for children with complex underlying causes.
preventionStrategies: Promote exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months of life and continued breastfeeding until at least 2 years alongside appropriate complementary feeding.
Introduce diverse complementary foods from 6 months that include protein-rich sources: legumes, eggs, fish, meat, or dairy, not just starchy staples.
Vaccinate against measles, which is a major precipitant of severe malnutrition; universal measles vaccination has reduced kwashiorkor incidence measurably in Africa.
Improve access to clean water, sanitation, and hand hygiene to reduce diarrhoeal disease burden and infection-related protein catabolism.
Implement community-based growth monitoring and promotion programmes to detect early nutritional faltering before severity progresses.
Provide targeted food supplementation and micronutrient supplementation during periods of food insecurity, drought, or conflict.
lifestyleModifications: Follow the treating team's feeding schedule strictly during recovery; do not overfeed between prescribed meals, as sudden excess intake can precipitate dangerous electrolyte shifts (refeeding syndrome).
Ensure adequate safe water access for preparation of therapeutic feeds and for handwashing.
Re-establish breastfeeding during recovery if the child is of appropriate age and the mother is able.
Maintain follow-up attendance at outpatient therapeutic programmes until weight-for-height is above -2 Z-scores and has been stable for at least two weeks.
Address household food security and complementary feeding practices with community health workers to prevent relapse after discharge.

recommended: F-75 therapeutic formula during inpatient stabilisation, given in frequent small volumes as prescribed
Ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF) during rehabilitation, consumed fully at each prescribed dose
Introduction of diverse household foods (eggs, legumes, fish, dairy) gradually after RUTF recovery phase
Breast milk continued at any age during and after treatment as a supplement to therapeutic feeding
avoid: High-volume oral fluid intake during stabilisation, which risks overloading a compromised heart
Iron supplementation during the first 14 days of treatment, which promotes bacterial growth during active infection
High-lactose foods in the initial phase, as gut mucosal damage causes temporary lactose intolerance
Standard WHO ORS for rehydration (use ReSoMal instead to avoid sodium overload)

exerciseGuidelines: Physical activity is severely restricted during the inpatient stabilisation phase because severely malnourished children have critically limited energy reserves and cardiovascular reserve. Structured play, sensory stimulation, and gentle physical interaction are provided from the earliest days to counteract the psychological withdrawal and delayed development associated with kwashiorkor. As recovery progresses into the rehabilitation phase, age-appropriate play and supervised physical activity are gradually reintroduced over 2–4 weeks alongside progressive nutritional rehabilitation.
dailyManagement: Monitor for signs of hypoglycaemia — sweating, shakiness, pallor, or unconsciousness — every 2–3 hours during inpatient stabilisation; feed immediately if any signs appear.
Check temperature regularly; cover the child to prevent hypothermia, particularly at night.
Record each feed taken, urine output, and daily weight (to track oedema resolution and weight gain).
Administer the full prescribed micronutrient supplement daily; do not substitute commercial multivitamins for the WHO protocol package.
After discharge, complete the full outpatient RUTF course and attend all scheduled follow-up visits to confirm sustained recovery.
prognosis: With prompt application of WHO inpatient protocol, case fatality rates for kwashiorkor have fallen to 5–10% in well-resourced treatment centres; untreated or inadequately treated kwashiorkor carries case fatality rates exceeding 30%. Children who survive severe acute malnutrition with adequate treatment generally recover normal anthropometric status within 6–12 weeks of outpatient nutritional rehabilitation. However, cognitive, neurodevelopmental, and immune sequelae can persist for years, with studies showing reduced school performance and working memory in previously severely malnourished children compared with adequately nourished peers. Relapse rates are high — approximately 10–20% within 6 months of discharge in community settings — if the underlying social, environmental, and dietary causes are not addressed alongside clinical treatment. Children who experienced multiple episodes of severe acute malnutrition in early childhood have measurably higher rates of stunting, reduced adult height, and reduced economic productivity.
recoveryTimeline: Oedema resolution is the primary early marker of response: bilateral pitting oedema typically begins to resolve within 4–7 days of starting F-75 and antibiotic treatment. Weight initially appears to fall as oedema fluid is excreted, then rises as genuine tissue is laid down during rehabilitation from day 7–14 onwards. Skin and hair changes improve over 2–4 weeks. Appetite usually returns within 1–2 weeks, signalling readiness for transition to F-100 or RUTF. The full outpatient RUTF rehabilitation course to achieve a weight-for-height above -2 Z-scores typically takes 6–10 weeks. Liver fat resolves over 1–3 months on adequate nutrition.
complications: Sepsis and bacteraemia — impaired immune function from protein and micronutrient deficiency makes severely malnourished children highly susceptible to overwhelming bacterial infection; mortality from sepsis in untreated kwashiorkor exceeds 20%.

Refeeding syndrome — aggressive nutritional rehabilitation shifts phosphate, potassium, and magnesium from blood into cells, causing life-threatening hypophosphataemia, cardiac arrhythmia, respiratory failure, and seizures; this is why F-75 starts slow and electrolytes are monitored closely.

Heart failure — the compromised myocardium of severe malnutrition cannot tolerate rapid intravenous fluid or aggressive oral rehydration; excess fluids cause pulmonary oedema and cardiac decompensation.
Hypoglycaemia — frequent, dangerous, and easily missed; it causes brain damage and death if not corrected within minutes, making blood glucose monitoring a cornerstone of initial management.

Permanent neurodevelopmental impairment — severe protein-energy malnutrition during the critical window of brain development (0–36 months) causes measurable reductions in cognitive function, school readiness, and adult earning potential.

Stunting and long-term growth failure — even after recovery from kwashiorkor, children who experienced severe acute malnutrition frequently do not achieve full height potential and face higher lifelong risks of metabolic disease.

supportResources.name: WHO — Management of Severe Acute Malnutrition in Infants and Children
supportResources.description: The WHO clinical protocol document that defines the 10-step management approach for kwashiorkor and severe acute malnutrition worldwide.

supportResources.name: UNICEF — Acute Malnutrition
supportResources.description: UNICEF overview of global acute malnutrition burden, treatment programmes, and advocacy resources for kwashiorkor and wasting in children.

supportResources.name: Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) — Nutrition Guidelines
supportResources.description: Practical clinical reference for severe acute malnutrition management in humanitarian settings including kwashiorkor treatment protocols.

sources.title: WHO — Guidelines for the Management of Severe Acute Malnutrition in Infants and Children Less than 5 Years (2013)
Bhutta ZA et al. Evidence-Based Interventions for Improvement of Maternal and Child Nutrition. Lancet, 2013
Trehan I, Manary MJ. Management of Severe Acute Malnutrition in Low-Income and Middle-Income Countries. Archives of Disease in Childhood, 2015
Briend A et al. Ready-to-Use Therapeutic Food for Treatment of Marasmus, Lancet, 1999
Golden MH. Proposed Recommended Nutrient Densities for Moderately Malnourished Children. Food and Nutrition Bulletin, 2009
UNICEF-WHO-World Bank Group — Joint Child Malnutrition Estimates, 2023 Edition
Waterlow JC. Classification and Definition of Protein-Calorie Malnutrition. BMJ, 1972

confusedWithConditions.name: Nutritional marasmus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Marasmus presents with obvious severe wasting and absence of oedema, giving the appearance of a shrunken 'old man face'; kwashiorkor has oedema that can mask wasting and make the child appear deceptively plump, though both are severe acute malnutrition requiring urgent treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anaemia with oedema
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Severe anaemia can cause facial and leg oedema from cardiac failure or hypoalbuminaemia, but is not associated with the skin and hair changes, hepatomegaly, or protein-poor dietary history characteristic of kwashiorkor; MUAC and serum albumin help differentiate.

confusedWithConditions.name: Unspecified severe protein-calorie malnutrition
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: This category is used when clinical features of severe malnutrition are present but insufficient information exists to distinguish kwashiorkor from marasmus or the mixed form; the presence of bilateral pitting oedema is the key criterion for classifying kwashiorkor specifically.

confusedWithConditions.name: Marasmic kwashiorkor
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Marasmic kwashiorkor combines both oedema and severe wasting simultaneously, indicating the most advanced and highest-risk form; pure kwashiorkor has oedema without the degree of visible wasting that defines the combined syndrome.

relatedConditions.name: Marasmic kwashiorkor
relatedConditions.relevance: The combined form of severe wasting and oedema; all children with kwashiorkor are at risk of progressing to or overlapping with marasmic kwashiorkor if caloric deprivation is added to protein deficit.

relatedConditions.name: Nutritional marasmus
relatedConditions.relevance: Marasmus and kwashiorkor represent the two poles of severe acute malnutrition; both require the same WHO 10-step treatment framework but differ in their predominant nutritional deficit and clinical presentation.

relatedConditions.name: Retarded development following protein-calorie malnutrition
relatedConditions.relevance: The cognitive and physical developmental sequelae of severe protein-energy malnutrition including kwashiorkor, recognising the long-term neurodevelopmental impact of malnutrition during the critical early-childhood window.

relatedConditions.name: Severe protein-calorie malnutrition (unspecified)
relatedConditions.relevance: The broader coding category under which kwashiorkor sits; when the distinction between oedematous and non-oedematous subtypes cannot be made clinically, E43 is used as the diagnostic code.

faqs.question: What is kwashiorkor?
faqs.answer: Kwashiorkor is a severe form of protein malnutrition that causes swelling (oedema) in the legs, arms, and face from fluid leaking into tissues. It affects mainly young children after weaning but can occur in any age group with prolonged protein-deficient intake.

faqs.question: What causes kwashiorkor?
faqs.answer: The primary cause is a diet severely deficient in protein relative to energy — typically starchy foods with little or no meat, eggs, fish, or legumes. Recurrent infections, early weaning from breast milk, and food insecurity are key contributing factors. Antioxidant deficiency may also play a role.

faqs.question: What are the signs of kwashiorkor?
faqs.answer: The defining sign is bilateral pitting oedema — swelling that leaves a dent when pressed firmly for 3 seconds. Other signs are skin peeling and discolouration, reddish or thin hair, a swollen belly from liver enlargement, extreme irritability, and loss of appetite.

faqs.question: Is kwashiorkor the same as starvation?
faqs.answer: No. Kwashiorkor is specifically a protein deficiency, not total starvation. A child can consume enough calories from starchy foods while still being severely protein-deficient. Starvation (marasmus) causes obvious wasting, while kwashiorkor causes swelling that can hide the underlying malnutrition.

faqs.question: How is kwashiorkor treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment follows a WHO 10-step protocol: correct hypoglycaemia and hypothermia immediately, treat infections with antibiotics, give small frequent feeds of F-75 therapeutic formula during stabilisation, then progress to F-100 or ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF) during rehabilitation.

faqs.question: Can kwashiorkor be cured?
faqs.answer: Yes, with prompt treatment most children make a full nutritional recovery. Case fatality rates drop from over 20% to below 5% with the WHO protocol. However, full recovery of height and neurodevelopmental potential takes much longer, and relapse is common if underlying food insecurity is not addressed.

faqs.question: How do you prevent kwashiorkor?
faqs.answer: Prevention requires exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months, introducing diverse protein-rich complementary foods from 6 months, vaccinating against measles, improving sanitation to reduce diarrhoeal disease, and addressing household food insecurity.

faqs.question: What is the difference between kwashiorkor and marasmus?
faqs.answer: Kwashiorkor is oedematous protein malnutrition — the child looks swollen and may appear a normal weight. Marasmus is severe caloric deficiency causing obvious wasting — the child looks thin and aged. Both are forms of severe acute malnutrition. The two conditions can overlap (marasmic kwashiorkor).

faqs.question: Why does kwashiorkor cause a swollen belly?
faqs.answer: The belly swelling comes from two sources: fluid accumulation in the abdomen (ascites) from low blood protein levels, and an enlarged fatty liver. Intestinal gas from bacterial overgrowth in the damaged gut also contributes to abdominal distension.

faqs.question: Is kwashiorkor only in Africa?
faqs.answer: No. Kwashiorkor was first described in Ghana and remains most common in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia, but it occurs in any setting with severe protein deficiency — including in adults with alcoholism, chronic intestinal disease, or advanced cancer in higher-income countries.

faqs.question: What is MUAC measurement?
faqs.answer: MUAC stands for mid-upper-arm circumference. It is measured with a coloured tape around the middle of the upper arm. A MUAC below 115 mm in a child aged 6–59 months indicates severe acute malnutrition requiring immediate referral. It is the main community screening tool for malnutrition.

faqs.question: What is ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF)?
faqs.answer: RUTF is a peanut butter-based, energy-dense paste fortified with vitamins and minerals used to treat severe acute malnutrition. It does not require mixing or refrigeration, is eaten directly from a sachet, and provides about 500 calories per packet. Each child receives a prescribed daily dose based on weight.

faqs.question: Why do children with kwashiorkor have reddish hair?
faqs.answer: Protein deficiency reduces melanin production in hair follicles, causing normal dark hair to lose pigmentation and turn reddish-brown or blonde. In children who have had repeated episodes of malnutrition, alternating bands of pale and normal hair appear — called the flag sign.

faqs.question: Is kwashiorkor dangerous without treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Untreated kwashiorkor carries case fatality rates of 20–30% in hospital series. Children die from sepsis, hypoglycaemia, hypothermia, and heart failure. Even survivors without treatment face permanent cognitive impairment and growth stunting.

faqs.question: What is refeeding syndrome?
faqs.answer: Refeeding syndrome is a dangerous shift in electrolytes — particularly a sharp fall in blood phosphate, potassium, and magnesium — that occurs when severely malnourished patients are fed too quickly after prolonged starvation. It can cause heart arrhythmia and death. WHO F-75 protocol avoids it by starting feeding slowly.

faqs.question: Can adults get kwashiorkor?
faqs.answer: Yes. Adults develop kwashiorkor from severe alcoholism, Crohn's disease, short bowel syndrome, advanced cancer, and prolonged hospitalisation on protein-poor intravenous fluids. It is underdiagnosed in adults because oedema is attributed to other causes rather than malnutrition.

faqs.question: How long does kwashiorkor take to recover from?
faqs.answer: Oedema usually begins resolving within 4–7 days of starting WHO treatment. Full nutritional recovery with RUTF typically takes 6–10 weeks. Cognitive and developmental recovery takes much longer and may be incomplete if malnutrition occurred before age 3.

faqs.question: Why is iron not given in the first two weeks of kwashiorkor treatment?
faqs.answer: During active infection, iron promotes bacterial growth and generates harmful free radicals in a child whose antioxidant defences are already depleted. WHO protocol defers iron supplementation until day 14 or later, when infections are controlled and antioxidant defences begin recovering.

faqs.question: What does the skin look like in kwashiorkor?
faqs.answer: The skin develops a 'flaky paint' appearance — areas of hypopigmentation (pale patches), darkening, peeling, and ulceration mainly on the legs, buttocks, and groin. The skin is fragile and breaks down easily. These changes improve gradually over 2–4 weeks of nutritional treatment.

faqs.question: Can kwashiorkor cause permanent brain damage?
faqs.answer: Severe protein-energy malnutrition during the first 1,000 days of life — from conception to age 2 — can permanently impair brain development. Studies show reduced cognitive scores, working memory, and school performance in children who had kwashiorkor compared with matched well-nourished children.

metaDescription: Kwashiorkor is severe protein malnutrition causing oedema, skin changes and liver swelling in children. Learn causes, WHO treatment protocol and prevention.
